Case Study

How Tesla Had to Secretly Buy Its Own Name

Before Tesla could sell a single car in China, it had to privately acquire the "Tesla" trademark from a squatter who had registered it years earlier.

Pre-entry
Acquisition Type
特斯拉
Chinese Name Acquired
Undisclosed
Acquisition Cost

The Quiet Acquisition

Before Tesla could sell a single car in China, it had to privately acquire the "Tesla" trademark from a Chinese squatter who had registered it years earlier. Unlike the dramatic public battles of Apple or Michael Jordan, Tesla's story played out quietly — but the lesson is just as important.

A Chinese businessman named Zhan Baosheng had registered "Tesla" (特斯拉) as a trademark in China and even set up a website (TeslaMotors.com.cn) claiming to be the official Chinese presence of Tesla Motors.

The Squatter's Strategy

Zhan Baosheng registered the "Tesla" trademark and the Chinese transliteration "Te Si La" (特斯拉) in China before Tesla Motors entered the market. He also:

  • Registered the domain TeslaMotors.com.cn
  • Set up social media accounts claiming to represent Tesla
  • Filed trademark applications across multiple product categories
  • Publicly offered to sell the trademark to Tesla

This is a classic "trademark squatting" strategy: identify promising foreign brands, register their names in China before they do, then wait for them to pay.

Tesla's Options

Tesla faced a difficult situation with three possible paths:

  • Option 1: Litigation. Challenge the registration in court. This could take years with uncertain outcomes, delaying Tesla's China entry.
  • Option 2: Negotiate. Buy the trademark from the squatter. Faster but expensive, and it rewards squatting behavior.
  • Option 3: Rebrand. Use a different name in China. Risky for a global brand with strong name recognition.

Tesla chose Option 2 — negotiate a private acquisition. The exact amount was never publicly disclosed, but industry estimates suggest it was significant.

The hidden cost: Most trademark disputes in China are resolved through expensive private deals, not public litigation. These deals are rarely publicized, but they represent a significant hidden cost of China market entry.

Key Lessons

  • Register before you announce. Tesla's plans to enter China were public knowledge long before they filed trademark applications. Squatters monitor international business news and act fast.
  • Domain names and trademarks are linked. The squatter didn't just register the trademark — he also grabbed the .com.cn domain, strengthening his negotiating position.
  • Private acquisitions are common but costly. Many international brands quietly buy trademarks from squatters before entering China.
  • The longer you wait, the more you pay. If Tesla had filed when it first started planning China operations, the cost would have been a few hundred dollars.
  • Multi-category registration prevents future problems. Even after acquiring the trademark, Tesla needed to ensure it was registered across all relevant categories.

How RTMCN Prevents This

At RTMCN, we recommend filing trademark applications at the earliest planning stage — before any public announcement. Our comprehensive search identifies existing registrations, and our direct CNIPA filing ensures your brand is secured before squatters can act.

Entering the Chinese market? Register your trademark before you announce your plans.
